posts.name: Andrew Heger
posts.cooked:
Wine Advocate rates the 2011 Champagne Vintage as 87 points.
Vinous says it is 70 points. 70. 70!

That’s a pretty big discrepancy.

Tasting a few (and mostly big names), I think Vinous is entirely right about this. So far, these range from comprehensively terrible (hello, Comtes!) to “This has a redeeming quality or two, but I would never pay anything near $115 for this mediocrity again (L-B VVdL).” Neither should have been made, nor others.

Anyone else experiencing this? At this point, if a heretofore unknown 2011 Salon suddenly appeared for $60, I literally wouldn’t buy it.

posts.name: Michael Hung
posts.cooked:
Andrew_Heger:

At this point, if a heretofore unknown 2011 Salon suddenly appeared for $60, I literally wouldn’t buy it.

I get what you’re trying to say, but I’m pretty positive that the majority of this forum (including me) would absolutely buy a 2011 Salon for $60 if it suddenly appeared

posts.name: Andrew Heger
posts.cooked: Yeah, ok, you got me. But only one tester bottle.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
I’m with you, 2011 Comtes is not good. Awkward is absolutely the best term for it both in the glass, and for whatever reason they decided to release it.

Please tell me there isn’t going to be a 2011 Salon? I thought it was curious enough they released 07

posts.name: Thomas De Waen
posts.cooked:
Think it very much depends what part of Champagne you’re talking about, as it’s a really big area. My personal experience is that most 2011s are problematic, but that it doesn’t apply to the Cote de Bars, which is geographically closer to Chablis than to Reims.

Having had the good fortune of recently tasting chez Cedric Bouchard, he pro-actively served a 2011 Haute Lemble (phenomenal wine!) and then went on to complain about how critics had lacked nuance about the vinatage… he felt that 2011 is actually a very good Cote de Bars vintage, and his full line up from that year supports that assertion.

-t

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Your point about the geography of Champagne is an excellent one. It’s a big area, and a lot of different soil types. I don’t think that enough is said about the differences region by region in general. While I hear your point, if I can only pick between more talk of geography or a region by region vintage chart, I’m going with the first. Has to start somewhere!

FWIW, I find that Bouchard largely defies any vintage chart in general. I can honestly say that I haven’t had a wine from him that I said “gee, this must have been vintage impacted” or “why did he make this wine?” My complaint generally is “damn, when did these wines get so pricey??”

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked:
Ian_Dorin:

FWIW, I find that Bouchard largely defies any vintage chart in general. I can honestly say that I haven’t had a wine from him that I said “gee, this must have been vintage impacted” or “why did he make this wine?” My complaint generally is “damn, when did these wines get so pricey??”

I agree. I actually don’t find a ton of vintage variation in his wines, which to me is fairly unusual for such small production and terroir-cognizant wine. (Then again, I also don’t find huge differences among his cuvees in the first place - they’re all good but they all taste more of Bouchard than whatever plot or grape it is.)

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: Agreed on the house style being imposed on the wines. Personally I find the wines heavily varietally influenced (which I think is cool), and gravitate towards the Pinot Noir based wines.

posts.name: Thomas De Waen
posts.cooked:
It’s not just Bouchard… The other big guns of the Aube (Vouette & Sorbee, Marie Courtin, Lassaigne), all produced fine to very fine 2011s.

Again, I agree that further north the 2011s are a bit of a disaster.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
I didn’t have Courtin’s 2011s, but have found them to be vintage dependent in the past, but talking about earlier vintages, like 05 and 06, so it’s possible the wines have moved away from that.

Vouette is always good for me

posts.name: Andrew Northup
posts.cooked:
I’ve only had a handful of 2011’s and looking at CT, these were my thoughts;

2011 Bouchard Boloree and VV - Agree with everyone’s comments above
2011 Charles Dufour Bistrotage Rose B.11 - I found this quite enjoyable as well
2011 Leclapart L’Apotre (twice) - I noted that it didn’t stand up to other vintages of Leclapart, but was itself perfectly enjoyable, though probably not for the price
2011 LB VVdL - I disagree with the OP on this one. I found my first bottle in early-ish 2021 to be a bit disjointed and just horribly young, but a bottle in March of this year showed very nicely with a little bit of air

Overall, probably easier for the growers to shake off the vintage vs. the large houses (none of which I’ve tried), though in some instances, while decent wines, they don’t quite live up to the label

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
The “discrepancy” in vintage rating could be explained in part by the sample set implied by the rating: the vintage was so bad many producers did not release 2011-specific bottlings, but perhaps those that did release made decent wines. If one took the first factor into account, the vintage score will be much lower than if only the second were taken into account.

I would also note that there is a very large discrepancy between Galloni and others regarding the 2011 Taittinger Comte. Galloni scored it a 75, whereas I’ve seen scores ranging from 92-97 from others.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: I think something that’s worth pointing out is that a number of the wines we are talking about are not technically vintage wines by Champagne standards. Could be something to be said about the fact they don’t go through as long of aging as required by Champagne rules, and that’s actually helping these wines be better. Food for thought if nothing else.

posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.cooked:
Andrew,

I also enjoyed the 2011 Larmandier-Bernier Champagne Grand Cru Vieille Vigne du Levant in March. I opened it at Brigg’s and FMlll’s Zoom tasting with Arthur Larmandier. Maybe you did the same. The farming, old vines, wood, malo, lees and more combine to make an extraordinary wine despite the challenging vintage. It’s rich, creamy, powerful and complex. Buttered almonds and hazelnut, bread in the oven, pear, brown spice, with nice cut and freshness. I thought it precocious for 11 years, drinking really well. I suspect it has the stuffing to go another ten. I purchased a couple of more bottles and magnums after that tasting.
That said, I didn’t buy much else from 2011 except Vilmart’s Couer de Cuvée. In my experience, Lauren Champs routinely excels even in tough vintages. I haven’t opened any yet, so I can’t say for sure he did it again in '11.
Many of the big house wines typically buy made it a NV year; DP, Cristal, and I believe Ruinart. I skipped the Comtes, although I had read positive notes from Jancis as well as Charles Curtis from Decanter.

I try not to paint in broad brushstrokes when it comes to vintage reports, especially for regions with varied climate, soils and winemaking.

Cheers,
Warren

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: If there is Val Vilane offered for 60 bucks, I am definitely going to buy it …we can only hope! (Sad to think that this used to be the price back when you couldn’t read the labels)

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: Any one know if Richard Juhlin rated it?

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked:
The 11s I have had (and still own a little) are both mentioned above. 11 L-B VV and 11 Vilmart CdC. I have enjoyed them both, and would buy more of each if I was backfilling. And, I would vote for Warren and Andrew as voices for whom, as much as any critic, I would look to for perspective.

  • 2011 Larmandier-Bernier Champagne Grand Cru Vieille Vigne du Levant Extra Brut - France, Champagne, Champagne Grand Cru (7/1/2022)
    Tasting w/ The Gang @ Counselor Seiber's Pad (Orange County, CA): Disgorged June 2019. 100% Chard with a few grams of dosage. This bottle reflected a strong almond quality, which I thought was pretty cool. And of course citrusy, infused with lemon and a light inflection of tropical fruit with some fennel in the finish. This vintage of VV is for me both elegant and yet has the L-B spiciness, in a medium weight. I do think that vintage and the 10 years that have passed already since harvest make the wine drink just fine right now, too.
  • 2011 Vilmart & Cie Champagne Premier Cru Coeur de Cuvée - France, Champagne, Champagne Premier Cru (2/14/2022)
    Disgorged May 2018, 80% Chard + 20% Pinot Noir. We finished this bottle over dinner within about 90 mins so it did not have a chance to flesh out completely as my last one did. Plenty of acid here, with some of the CdC signature creaminess and some of the tropical character of the previous bottles. This showed fine and while it doesn't have the depth of the better years, this is still damn good.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked: I’m just glad 2010 and 2011 are well into the rear view mirror. I remember going to a big tasting in 2014 or thereabouts and the NVs were rough sledding.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I guess there are two points to consider:

  1. What’s the real difference between 87 and 70? Does 87/100 suggest a great vintage? Vinous has rated around 75 Champagnes from the 2011 vintage, the lowest scoring being 75 points and the highest scoring being 96. Just over 25 were scores in the 80s (mostly 88/100), and only two were scored in the 70s. That leaves just under 50 Champagnes with reviews in the 90s. If the average Vinous score for 2011 vintage Champagnes is considerably higher than 70/100, in what sense can the vintage be considered to merit 70/100?

  2. A more fundamental question: Do any serious wine lovers actually take “ratings” for an entire vintage in an entire region seriously? To me, if you are even consulting such things, well… Vintage charts date from the pre-internet era. With extensive searchable databases, you can find much more meaningful commentary on specific wines without needing to resort to the ludicrously broad generalizations offered by vintage charts. There’s a reason our vintage chart is in front of our paywall…

posts.name: Keith A k e r s
posts.cooked:
I’ve basically avoided 11s the past few years. The PP Les Chets was enjoyable, but it wasn’t at the quality I normally expect. Same with the Hebrart SC.

I have to go through all of my notes over the years, but I definitely noticed a green/herbal streak in a lot of northern 11s that was off-putting (the 11 Campania-Remensis was tough going and the 11 Laval Rose was really bad). Too much good champagne to buy to worry about a less than stellar vintage like 11 that really should’ve been blended out in a lot of places.
posts.display_username: Keith A k e r s

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
Ian_Dorin:

Please tell me there isn’t going to be a 2011 Salon? I thought it was curious enough they released 07

Lol, no way Salon releases a 2011. At least with 2007 chardonnay was generally less affected by weather conditions and had pretty good and pretty even ripeness, all things considered. I think there are a good number of very solid BdB from the vintage, and Dom Ruinart is a delight (if not as powerful and complete as 2002). Even with 2004 being so solid for champagne, I’d say the 2007 Dom Ruinart holds its own against 2004.

Just over 25 were scores in the 80s (mostly 88/100), and only two were scored in the 70s. That leaves just under 50 Champagnes with reviews in the 90s. I gave a possible explanation to that question above. Note that I am aware of the epistemological issues associated with basing judgments on things that never existed.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

I guess there are two points to consider:

  1. What’s the real difference between 87 and 70? Does 87/100 suggest a great vintage? Vinous has rated around 75 Champagnes from the 2011 vintage, the lowest scoring being 75 points and the highest scoring being 96. Just over 25 were scores in the 80s (mostly 88/100), and only two were scored in the 70s. That leaves just under 50 Champagnes with reviews in the 90s. If the average Vinous score for 2011 vintage Champagnes is considerably higher than 70/100, in what sense can the vintage be considered to merit 70/100?
  2. A more fundamental question: Do any serious wine lovers actually take “ratings” for an entire vintage in an entire region seriously? To me, if you are even consulting such things, well… Vintage charts date from the pre-internet era. With extensive searchable databases, you can find much more meaningful commentary on specific wines without needing to resort to the ludicrously broad generalizations offered by vintage charts. There’s a reason our vintage chart is in front of our paywall…

Decanter’s lad rated 2011 1/5, and yet I’m guessing the average rating wasn’t a 25/100 or 4/20. And yet it gets the message across that if you’re perusing vintages, there is no vintage you should try harder to avoid in recent years than 2011. I’d say Vinous’s score of 70 gives that same message. That their scores of actual wines indicate differently is a different issue I have with Vinous.

I think most people see 87 the way they see 3/5 or 4/5. Oh, a decent vintage that’s probably pretty okay from most of the top half of quality producers. Never mind that there are probably 4 people on the entire “What champagne are you drinking” thread who’ve had an 87 point rated champagne on their own accord in the past five years.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
You really consider 87/100 to be equivalent to 4/5? That leaves me perplexed.

Note that I didn’t write this part of the vintage chart, nor the older ratings, so I am not particularly invested in it; but in the current world of score inflation/compression, I am pretty sure that people understand that 87/100 is not intended to convey glowing enthusiasm but rather “a weak vintage as a whole in which a few interesting wines were produced”. Equally, I have a real objection to writing off whole vintages, which simply doesn’t reflect the reality of what modern agronomy and meticulous selection can deliver (like vintage charts themselves, this is a mentality from the 1980s, when everything was picked at once and put in the same tank). There are some good 2011 Champagnes, from the Aube and a few top producers in the Côte des Blancs, as well as the likes of Vilmart, and it makes no sense to ignore them.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: To answer point 2, hell no.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
I love 07 Ruinart. Such a great bottle to surprise people with the vintage.

Have you had 2007 Salon? It’s fine, but not a vintage that by tasting you would say is a must own, especially at current market price.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

You really consider 87/100 to be equivalent to 4/5? That leaves me perplexed.

Note that I didn’t write this part of the vintage chart, nor the older ratings, so I am not particularly invested in it; but in the current world of score inflation/compression, I am pretty sure that people understand that 87/100 is not intended to convey glowing enthusiasm but rather “a weak vintage as a whole in which a few interesting wines were produced”. Equally, I have a real objection to writing off whole vintages, which simply doesn’t reflect the reality of what modern agronomy and meticulous selection can deliver (like vintage charts themselves, this is a mentality from the 1980s, when everything was picked at once and put in the same tank). There are some good 2011 Champagnes, from the Aube and a few top producers in the Côte des Blancs, as well as the likes of Vilmart, and it makes no sense to ignore them.

No on in the states has been using a 5 star system. One could make the easy argument that 4/5 = 80, and 87 is higher than 80. But if you’re talking movies, or restaurants, a 4 star review is a positive. I’d probably think 87 was more like a 3/5 in the wine world.

There is easy confusion here, though. If you just see 87 from Wine Advocate on its own, it doesn’t seem very damning. That’s about what 96 Bordeaux got in a bunch of regions, with the exception of Paullac, St. Julien, St. Estephe. Other regions have many low 80s, high 70s rankings in off vintages, especially in prior decades. But you see vintage scores in the 70s and low 80s recently for Loire in 13, Mosel in 13 and 14, Napa in 2011. 87, standing alone, would be one of the best vintages from New Zealand.

It takes a look purely at champagne to realize that an 87 is one of the lowest ever vintage scores, placing it above 2006 and way above 2007, but those aside, generally worse than almost every other vintage going pretty far back.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Ian_Dorin:

To answer point 2, hell no.

But don’t they? Maybe not the numerical rating, but broad vintage generalizations for sure. People, even wine board frequenters, like things easy. It’s so much easier to be told “buy this vintage” or “skip this vintage.” Maybe they tell themselves is just risk mitigation - if there’s a limited amount of money to spend, why spend it in a weak vintage? - but some of the biggest buyers on this board quite vociferously declare how they are skipping the whole vintage, or that a whole vintage is crap, often without tasting, or tasting just one of two wines. There are also people who are afraid of looking or feeling stupid for buying in a lesser vintage. Not everyone, obviously, and probably less so here than elsewhere, but it’s all over the place, even all over Berserkers.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
I was answering the question about ratings and how it pertains to a vintage. People make their own judgements. They aren’t looking at someone’s vintage chart to make that assumption. Easy case in point, how often does someone on this board say “well, it’s an 87 point vintage, so I’m out”. If someone had, I’m sure they were being told all the reasons that is foolish.

Plus, when you are talking about serious collectors, if you have an allocation of DRC at the right price, you aren’t going to skip it, right? Or Selosse (to keep with the Champagne theme)?

It’s interesting this came up on a Champagne discussion. The biggest houses don’t make their top wines every year.

posts.name: M. Ch@o
posts.cooked: This is some of the most rating-heavy discussion I’ve seen in a while. Here is how I would sum up 2011 or any “bad” vintage assuming I’ve tried enough to form my own opinion: I won’t go out of my way to buy or try wines from the vintage, but if a wine I haven’t tried is put in front of me, I won’t cast it aside.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: Buried in here is good discussion about the geography of Champagne and how it can make a big difference

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Ian_Dorin:

Buried in here is good discussion about the geography of Champagne and how it can make a big difference

Definitely. And I don’t find vintage generalizations to be particularly illuminating. So I try to judge what’s in the bottle according to my own preferences. That said, I’ve had both 2011 Bouchard Haute Lemble and Larmandier-Bernier Levant within the last week, and I found both to be tough sailing. Not for me. Whereas Vilmart Couer de Cuvee was wonderful the couple times I’ve had it.

Champagne preferences are so idiosyncratic.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked: All this talk about champagne has me thinking I’ll toss a 2008 Lanson on ice when I get home and check in on it.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Jayson_Cohen:

Champagne preferences are so idiosyncratic.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

I guess there are two points to consider:

  1. What’s the real difference between 87 and 70? Does 87/100 suggest a great vintage? Vinous has rated around 75 Champagnes from the 2011 vintage, the lowest scoring being 75 points and the highest scoring being 96. Just over 25 were scores in the 80s (mostly 88/100), and only two were scored in the 70s. That leaves just under 50 Champagnes with reviews in the 90s. If the average Vinous score for 2011 vintage Champagnes is considerably higher than 70/100, in what sense can the vintage be considered to merit 70/100?

  2. A more fundamental question: Do any serious wine lovers actually take “ratings” for an entire vintage in an entire region seriously? To me, if you are even consulting such things, well… Vintage charts date from the pre-internet era. With extensive searchable databases, you can find much more meaningful commentary on specific wines without needing to resort to the ludicrously broad generalizations offered by vintage charts. There’s a reason our vintage chart is in front of our paywall…

There are times IMHO that vintage charts convey information and times IMHO where they do not. I am not sure how to use a vintage chart, for example, to compare 2015, 2016 and 2017 vintages for red Burgundies. Lots of excellent wines made in the three vintages, but in very different styles.

But, vintage charts should highlight one to a disaster vintage (like 2004 in Red Burgundy) and to a truly exceptional vintage (like 2014 in white Burgundy).

But, vintage charts should highlight one to a disaster vintage (like 2004 in Red Burgundy) and to a truly exceptional vintage (like 2014 in white Burgundy).

“Disaster vintage”, yet wine searcher says:

“The 2004 vintage for Burgundy was good but there were some difficulties.”

I think vintage charts are mostly fake news. Too many generalizations it seems.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
2011 is not a good vintage in Champagne. Going back to 1980, it is probably the worst except for 1984, 1994, and 2001. It is also the only poorer vintage since 1980 that I blame not only on nature, but on poor harvesting choices. That said, it isn’t a washout and there are some good wines in the vintage. You just have to or had to taste first. The earliest released NVs based on 2011 were rough going as they were hard and often had a green edge. Lees aging helped out those who were released later, but most NVs from 2011 were rough upon release. Over time, I have seen the harder, greener notes fade and they are much better now, but still probably below average when compared to most releases.

I don’t think there was really any region that excelled in 2011. Chardonnay clearly did the best, but Chardonnay normally does the best in poor vintages which is why you can find some decent Chardonnays or Chardonnay heavy wines in 1994, 2001, and 2010. 1997 and 2007 are a couple other examples of Chardonnay years in vintages that were not all that well regarded. The key in 2011 was to wait to harvest and often do multiple passes over multiple days in the same vineyard or locale; the problem was that few did this. There was still a lot of harvesting based on numbers and fear in 2011. Few paid attention to the ups/downs and extremes of the growing season which should have been a clue to look into more than just numbers on paper. On paper, the numbers said to harvest early or a few days after opening. As the harvest opened, the grapes often did not have ripe phenolics, or when they did, a site was quite uneven in terms of ripeness (especially Chardonnay). Ripening also really slowed down right when harvest opened. Additionally, there was a general fear of rain/rot/mold and another 2010. This led many to chose to not wait or stop waiting and just start picking everything.

If people had waited and did multiple waves of harvesting in locations, the grapes did ripen well especially the Chardonnays and even the Pinots in most of the top villages. The problem is that most didn’t. 2011 was never going to be a great vintage, but it should have been better than it was and it should have been better overall than 2010. It probably could have been as good as 2007; this may not sound like anything to write home about, but the region would have been happy with another 2007 over 2011.

The big challenge with the poorer vintages in Champagne is really with larger producers or any producer’s large volume wines. You can always make some very good small quantity wines in any year, but if you need to try and make something in larger volume, it can be a challenge. The larger, better producers can often use reserve wines to blend away lesser vintage characteristics, but you can only do some much. Additionally, the larger producers are often limited in making larger quantity vintage wines in poor years so they skip them. For smaller producers, they can often make some really tasty vintage and/or site specific wines, but in small volumes. Many small producers really struggled with NVs in 2011 because they didn’t have a large array or reserves and had to count on wines from 2010 that weren’t all that great either.

Media also played a part in what was released with a 2011 label. With all of the talk of 2011 being poor, a number of producers decided to not release planned 2011s as they feared it wouldn’t go over well especially after really good releases from 2008/2009 and the high quality planned releases from 2012/2013. They didn’t want negative criticism on the brand or top wines.

Of other recent vintages of note that some will talk of being poor, 2010 and 2017 have or will have some amazing Chardonnay wines. 2021 is more a low quantity, classical year than a bad year. Champagne has learned a lot over the last 30 years and how they harvest has changed. There is a lot more emphasis on ripe phenolics than acidity/potential alcohol numbers and much more picking in waves and starts/stops than in the past. If 2011 were to have happened this year, the wines would have been much better. Conversely, if 2022 were to have happened in 2011, I think we would have seen a much less promising vintage than the one we appear to have.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Champagne, as a region, is larger than the country of Luxembourg. There’s going to be variation! The problem with 2011s is generally a green herbal streak - Marguet’s Sapience, Bouchard’s Creux d’Enfer, Comtes, etc. But lots of good wine too - people have mentioned Larmandier-Bernier and Vilmart already, but also Agrapart, Egly (who made a vintage cuvée, etc).
It’s not a vintage to buy blind, but especially in champagne vintage charts seem pretty meaningless to me. It’s not like 2004 red burgundy where there are rumors of 3 producers who did well.

I’ve only had a handful of 2011’s and looking at CT, these were my thoughts;

2011 Bouchard Boloree and VV - Agree with everyone’s comments above

2011 Leclapart L’Apotre (twice) - I noted that it didn’t stand up to other vintages of Leclapart, but was itself perfectly enjoyable, though probably not for the price
2011 LB VVdL - I disagree with the OP on this one. I found my first bottle in early-ish 2021 to be a bit disjointed and just horribly young, but a bottle in March of this year showed very nicely with a little bit of air

Overall, probably easier for the growers to shake off the vintage vs. the large houses (none of which I’ve tried), though in some instances, while decent wines, they don’t quite live up to the label

I had the 2011 Boloree for a birthday dinner last week, and it’s still great. Just don’t drink his 11 rose

Cheers,
Warren

Comtes is one of my favorite house champagnes, but the 2011 isn’t great. Cristal actually made a 2011 that they chose not to release.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I would think Champagne would be a region where it would be harder to use a vintage chart.

First, I think of it as a large region, so what is a good or bad vintage one place would not be a good or bad vintage someplace else.

Then, second, there are primarily three different varieties of grapes. I know from Burgundy that the good years for Pinot Noir and the good years for Chardonnay are not necessarily the same. For example, 2014 is a great vintage for white Burgs and a very good vintage for red Burgundies. I would have to think in Champagne that results could vary in a vintage depending on the varieties used.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
A producer who really fascinates me is Guiborat. His Prisme 2011 was one of the best champagnes of the vintage. Curiously he did not even bother vinifying the 2012 but sold the grapes to Perrier Jouet, as they were not up to his standard.

Last year we held a tasting with a lot of big champagnes. Egly’s 2011 and Blanc de Noir based on the 2011 vintage are exceptional champagnes and performed at a level of Krug NV.

Also of great interest is Jacquesson’s 739 and degorgement tardif 739, both are still going strong.

I think champagne is more about the producer than the vintage. One sees this in the 2017 vintage on paper a disaster, butMarguet did not release crus and a lot of the cru juice went into his Shaman 17 is fantastic. Similiar thing with Leclapart, one big champagne L’Aphrodisiaque which for me is one of the best releases this year.

Vouette also really strong in the 2011 vintage.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
[quote=“D_Pennet, post:42, topic:291549, full:true”]Last year we held a tasting with a lot of big champagnes. Egly’s 2011 and Blanc de Noir based on the 2011 vintage are exceptional champagnes and performed at a level of Krug NV.
[/quote]

Which Krug NV? Since the 164, this isn’t really a big compliment. I’d expect a vintage Egly to be better than Krug MV.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

Marguet did not release crus and a lot of the cru juice went into his Shaman 17 is fantastic

Marguet released at least some crus in 2017. I don’t know about all, but I own and have drunk several.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
It was actually the Krug 167. I did not want to put it in but as their were some participants who had never drunk Krug, I had to.

We also had the Jacquesson 738 degorgement tardif and my feeling was there is not much different in quality (style yes) between Jacquesson, Egly and Krug. Some particpants felt Jacquesson was the better champagne, other the Egly Blanc de Noir.

The vintage Krug 2006 was at a different level. The real winner in the tasting was the Drappier ‘Grande Sendree’ Brut Millesime 2010 which with its price point was exceptional.

Sarah_Kirschbaum:

Marguet released at least some crus in 2017.

It looks like at least Verzenay, Ambonnay, Ambonay Rose, Les Crayeres, Les Parc BdB…

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

It was actually the Krug 167. I did not want to put it in but as their were some participants who had never drunk Krug, I had to.

We also had the Jacquesson 738 degorgement tardif and my feeling was there is not much different in quality (style yes) between Jacquesson, Egly and Krug. Some particpants felt Jacquesson was the better champagne, other the Egly Blanc de Noir.

The vintage Krug 2006 was at a different level. The real winner in the tasting was the Drappier ‘Grande Sendree’ Brut Millesime 2010 which with its price point was exceptional.

If we’re discussing vintage Egly, I’d expect a sound bottle (and there is bottle variation) to be better than any Krug past 2004 vintage/164 MV. The Egly vintage is, imho, one of the flagship wines in all of champagne. I’m not trying to do a hot take here.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Anyone had a 96 E-O recently. Not a great success IMHO.

Russell_Faulkner:

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: Burgundy is usually rated by region not just a wide swipe at all Burgundy. If a publication wants to be serious re. Champagne they need to rate region and variety in said region. Its why I enjoy Brad Baker so much as he will discuss region and strength of Pinot Noir or Chard you don’t see elsewhere.

posts.name: Fred R
posts.cooked:
Interesting, I checked the database I have no notes on 2011. I’ll be opening a 2012 on Thurs.

But I do have nearly a case of 2011 bought on a recommendation I trust, not tasted yet, but it was not expensive so as long as it does as well as any reasonable NV I’m happy.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Yeah, the 96 matured and just kind of fell apart like a lot of Pinot heavy 96s did.

In general, I have found Egly’s vintage wine to be the most inconsistent of the range and often playing on the edge in terms of volatility. It just seems to sometimes push to far in terms of ripeness, extraction, oak, low sulfulr, and low dosage with the end result being lifted characteristics that can trend towards nail polish, slippery, and cidery aromas/flavors. I do really enjoy some vintages, but not all and not necessarily in the vintages I thought I would most.

The recent Bisseuil release was also a head scratcher to me with a lack of fruit, too much oak, and volatility.

The above being said, I find the rest of the range to be very enjoyable and fairly consistent.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Brad_Baker:

Yeah, the 96 matured and just kind of fell apart like a lot of Pinot heavy 96s did.

In general, I have found Egly’s vintage wine to be the most inconsistent of the range and often playing on the edge in terms of volatility. It just seems to sometimes push to far in terms of ripeness, extraction, oak, low sulfulr, and low dosage with the end result being lifted characteristics that can trend towards nail polish, slippery, and cidery aromas/flavors. I do really enjoy some vintages, but not all and not necessarily in the vintages I thought I would most.

The recent Bisseuil release was also a head scratcher to me with a lack of fruit, too much oak, and volatility.

The above being said, I find the rest of the range to be very enjoyable and fairly consistent.

I tend to ascribe the vintage issues to bottle variation, personally - I’ve had wines from the same case show fairly differently. I agree with you on the Bisseuil. My second favorite wine in the range is consistently the rose, which I think is consistently great.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Greg,

I don’t disagree on the bottle variation. I think some vintages show it more than others, but the volatility and variation shouldn’t be there especially on the ‘flagship’ wine (which is now the vintage). There is no reason these wines should not be more consistent. When they are good, they are good, but I think some of the winemaking and picking choices are getting quite risky. This isn’t just a problem with Egly, but a problem more and more in Champagne as many are pushing the limit. It is one thing for a new producer just starting out to push, make mistakes, and learn, but there are a number of hot/trendy producers who I feel made better wine 5-10+ years ago when they weren’t as popular. Maybe the problem is me.

posts.name: Ethan Abraham
posts.cooked: On the Vinous board AG commented that his score was the score for the year, and lots of producers didn’t release wines. So I read this as saying as the 70 point score is the score for the year “as a year to make wine” in some sense, not a score for the wines that were released.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I would venture that the real issue was not so much the raw materials as the use of cork-lined crown caps, which many growers used in this period, and which ended up being a much more oxidative closure for wine sur lattes than regular corks or the crown caps of today would have been. Egly himself told me that he regretted not disgorging all his wines that had seen tirage under cork-lined crown caps, as they simply cannot age for a long time sur lattes with that closure. Since the late 1990s, you have better crown caps; and then, in the late 2000s, you have Egly’s new press—that makes sense of the two step changes you see in the wines and their capacity to age. But they have nothing to do with the winemaking style or the élevage of the vins clairs.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
In its own terms, that makes sense; but 1) surely when consumers look at a vintage chart they are looking for guidance on vintaged wines and 2) how do you assess the quality of the vintage other than by tasting finished wines from that vintage?

When I started reviewing Champagne, there were a number of people who encouraged me to taste vast amounts of vins clairs, but I almost immediately came to see that this was a pointless exercise and at this stage only do it out of politeness. The idea that you can judge the quality of a vintage by tasting a few hundred base wines at Roederer, Krug and one or two growers is a joke. As the old English saying goes, the proof of the pudding is in the eating, the 2013 vintage being a great case in point.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Ethan_A:

On the Vinous board AG commented that his score was the score for the year, and lots of producers didn’t release wines. So I read this as saying as the 70 point score is the score for the year “as a year to make wine” in some sense, not a score for the wines that were released.

Is he rating it for people looking to buy barrels of grapes to make wine with? What an odd perspective for a consumer facing publication.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Brad_Baker:

Greg,

I don’t disagree on the bottle variation. I think some vintages show it more than others, but the volatility and variation shouldn’t be there especially on the ‘flagship’ wine (which is now the vintage). There is no reason these wines should not be more consistent. When they are good, they are good, but I think some of the winemaking and picking choices are getting quite risky. This isn’t just a problem with Egly, but a problem more and more in Champagne as many are pushing the limit. It is one thing for a new producer just starting out to push, make mistakes, and learn, but there are a number of hot/trendy producers who I feel made better wine 5-10+ years ago when they weren’t as popular. Maybe the problem is me.

I’d be quite curious about your list Brad! I think the producers I like have gone from strength to strength, but then I wouldn’t buy them otherwise!

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
William,

I know that you are not as big on the vins clairs as I am, but I think you can tell a heck of a lot by tasting from the fall to spring after harvest and seeing how the wines develop. You need to taste all over and include big, medium, and large folks, but I think you can tell all you need to know in terms of general quality across the regions, villages, grapes, etc… From tasting the vins clairs, the only surprise vintage (in terms of better/worse quality than expected) I can recall is 2009 and that is the only year since 2006 (other than 2020) where I didn’t taste an appropriate amount and array of vins clairs to form a complete opinion. The volcano eruption cancelled my spring 2010 trip and I was left with only tastings in the fall of 2009 and not enough of them to get a clear picture. 2020 was COVID cancelled for me and tasting a few wines through the mail doesn’t really count. I’m told 2020 is a good year and I have had some reserve wines, but I haven’t tasted enough to truly know.

I don’t quite understand the 2013 reference. 2013 showed great, classical potential as vins clairs with the Chardonnays showing the best especially in the Côte des Blancs. The southern Montagne de Reims and Marne Valley villages of Bisseuil through Cumières showed well for Chardonnay, Pinot Meunier, and Pinot Noir. Mailly, Verzenay, and Verzy along with the Côte des Bars were weak spots. This was all apparent in the vins clairs.
I love this thread. Thanks to all for the knowledge drops!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Well, my objections to tasting vins clairs are as follows:

  1. one tastes a highly curated selection of possible component of putative blends
  2. the wines have only just finished their fermentation and are often not completely dry, which makes it hard to meaningfully assess their structure and balance
  3. the final product is so far removed in time, process, and style from the vins clairs that tasting the latter only gives vague indications about the likely profile of the former
  4. it takes a lot of time and fatigues the palate

Do you feel that tasting vins clairs has ever told you anything about a wine that you wouldn’t have been able to perceive by tasting finished wines? For me, that’s really the heart of the matter.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked: He released a few but not nothing near the plethora of Crus he usually releases. A lot of the cru juice in 17 went into the Shaman

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
William,

There is a window of months to taste vins clairs both as components and sometimes as final blends so I don’t think the ‘just finished’ fermentation comment necessarily applies. I think you have to taste shortly after the harvest and then again in the spring to get a full picture. Some wines are still fermenting or going through malo right now so you can’t taste them and tasting right now is not the best time to get a full picture of 2022. It can give you some of it and it starts to form the general picture, but pieces are still missing and you really need tasting in March/April to get a complete view IMO.

I almost never taste vins clairs to try and get a feeling for a specific wine. This can be done, but is much more of a crapshoot and I think takes a lot of experience. To do this, you have to be able to link the final wine to the not the vins clairs, but 99% of the time the final vin clair blend for the wine and then you have to follow multiple years/releases of a wine from vin clair blend to the release. You can then start to equate how a vin clair blend will perform as a specific final wine, but I agree that this is really more of an educational activity than anything else. Dosage and disgorgement handling can so drastically effect a wine that this is a mine field.

As for tasting a curated selection of vins clairs, I think most do taste this way, but I have never had a problem asking to taste different wines from the ‘best’ and actually taste quite a bit of taille and lesser regarded wines. When tasting a year, when you get a chance with a producer, you have to taste steel, old oak, newer oak, malo, no malo, top site, lesser site, taille, wines meant for single plot, wines meant for vintage, wines meant for NV, wines meant for reserve etc… to form a proper picture.

What I think the vins clairs are good for is the general direction/quality of the year, grapes, regions, villages, specific areas, etc… You can get a great feeling for that. I remember tasting 2008 and clearly seeing that Pinot Meunier was sensational in the year. In 2013, the Cote des Blancs Chardonnays were singing. 2015 saw many wines exhibit lots of fruit with a spiciness or slightly herbal note that I found interesting. In 2018, the wines seemed spectacular, juicy, and energetic in late November of 2018, but had lost some energy and regressed a touch by the Spring of 2019. The 2019 wines had the most amazing mix of aromatics, fruit, and texture that I had ever tried and this was true for pretty much any grape, grown anywhere. 2021 is a very down talked vintage, but the wines are quite classical in nature, Chardonnay did well, the Cote des Blancs and surrounding villages did well. The main issue is quantity and grapes of the western Marne Valley and the Vallee de Ardre. The year of 2021 is not going to be spectacular, but I don’t think it will be as bad as many say; it has surprising upside.

Tasting vins clairs isn’t a substitute for tasting the final wine. The vins clairs give you general direction for vintage and also how recent years blend together. For example, I find insight from vins clairs in how well the current and previous vintage do when blended.

I get you on the time aspect and the palate fatigue. When in Champagne, I spend a large majority of my time tasting vins clairs and you have to prepare and plan for it. The final bottled Champagne can be tasted anywhere. I’m not saying tasting vins clairs is the get all or that everyone needs to or should do it. You certainly can get all you need to know by tasting the final released product, but I think you learn something additional and get some early information on the vintage and wines from the vins clairs that adds value to not only tasting the final released product, but searching out new Champagnes to try.

Just my 2 cents. There is no right or wrong answer here.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked: With the new releases with Egly this year all the bottles we received were degorged in August 2022, a recipe for a lot of bottle variaiton. I don’tthink he can keep up with demand. A second round will be released in January.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: It’s not you.

posts.name: Tom Mann
posts.cooked:
IIRC correctly, I remember Laurent telling me he deliberately waited to harvest as long as possible to let the grapes dry out and gain more ripeness (something which Brad references), and that he noticed a lot of vineyards who made bigger volumes just didn’t have that option. I don’t know if he made a 1994 but I know he made a 2001 and a very successful 1991.

(note - I list Vilmart)

user_status.description: ITB - wine merchant in the UK

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Tom,

Yes, the only year in recent memory where there was no vintage wine from Vilmart is 1994 (before that, maybe 1987?). Oddly, there wasn’t much 1995 made either as Laurent didn’t like the vintage at the time. Creation was made in 1995, but no Coeur de Cuvee. I’ve been told a small amount of Grand Cellier d’Or was made, but I have never had a bottle or asked Laurent about it.

“I didn’t have Courtin’s 2011s, but have found them to be vintage dependent in the past, but talking about earlier vintages, like 05 and 06, so it’s possible the wines have moved away from that.”

Wow… you’re a harsh judge. Dominique Moreau only founded Marie Courtin in 2005 and wasn’t really fully established/up and running until 2008 or thereabouts…

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Well, my objections to tasting vins clairs are as follows:

  1. one tastes a highly curated selection of possible component of putative blends
  2. the wines have only just finished their fermentation and are often not completely dry, which makes it hard to meaningfully assess their structure and balance
  3. the final product is so far removed in time, process, and style from the vins clairs that tasting the latter only gives vague indications about the likely profile of the former
  4. it takes a lot of time and fatigues the palate

Do you feel that tasting vins clairs has ever told you anything about a wine that you wouldn’t have been able to perceive by tasting finished wines? For me, that’s really the heart of the matter.

This… 90% of the time a pure waste of time, unless, for some weird reason, you are writing a PhD on vins clair, 10% of the time a mildly amusing, but still quite academic, exercise (Jacques Diebolt is one of those very few people who can actually make it seem… almost fun )

posts.name: Jamie Goode
posts.cooked: If you have no other information to go on, then vintage is a useful filter. But in a large region, and with a good vineyard and cellar team, you can produce something exceptional in pretty much every vintage if the selection is good enough. So aside from big production wines, then maybe the smart people scour the written-off vintages for gems? At least these days (I’m aware of some of the horror shows from the 1970s and 1980s…)

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: When I had them, I had no idea they were the first two vintages!

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
2011 Dom Ruinart is really, really good too. Beautiful, linear architecture with a firm, mineral spine and a hint of citrus and herbs. Yum.

EDIT: Whoops, I actually had 2010. Misremembered the vintage. Pardon the thread drift.

posts.name: Eric Tomasi
posts.cooked: So what you are saying is the 2011 Krug may not be worth the $300+ price that I’ve gotten emails about?

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked: 2011 Krug is actually a very nice wine and a more classically styled vintage for Krug. If you talk to the Krug folks, they will tell you it reminds them a bit of the elegant, more racy style of 2004 mixed in with some of the 2006’s richness. I can see where they get this thought from. I find a few similarities to the 1995 as well only with not as much minerality and a bit more of a richer, biscuity note. The 2011 Krug is more ready to drink right now than the 2008. Time on the lees has benefited a number of 2011s and this is a good example. In my book Krug gets a lot of credit for not only showing that you can make a good 2011, but also ignoring the bad press that 2011 got which scared others away from releasing.
